perturbation.original_text: In connection with an evaluation relating to a potential relationship, cooperation or transaction (the “Evaluation”), SAP and Company may deliver to each other, upon the execution of this Agreement, Confidential Information as defined below (the party disclosing such Confidential Information being the “Disclosing Party” and the party receiving such Confidential Information being the “Receiving Party”).
perturbation.changed_text: In connection with an evaluation relating to a potential relationship, cooperation or transaction (the “Assessment”), SAP and Company may deliver to each other, upon the execution of this Agreement, Confidential Information as defined below (the party disclosing such Confidential Information being the “Disclosing Party” and the party receiving such Confidential Information being the “Receiving Party”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Evaluation' to 'Assessment' in this specific instance creates ambiguity, because the rest of the document stills makes reference to the old term. It is not clear if the terms are meant to be the same, creating a contradiction between this section and other places where 'Evaluation' is mentioned such as section 3: 'Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of the Evaluation'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party: (a) shall take all reasonable steps (defined below) to keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential; (b) shall not disclose or reveal any Confidential Information to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of the Evaluation; (c) shall not use Confidential Information for any purpose other than in connection with the Evaluation. and (d) shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of the Evaluation) any information about the Evaluation, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives. As used herein “reasonable steps” means those steps the Receiving Party takes to protect its own similar proprietary and confidential information, which shall not be less than a reasonable standard of care. As used herein. “Representatives” shall mean (i) employees of Receiving Party; (ii) attorneys, accountants, or other professional business advisors and, additionally, (iii) employees of the Receiving Party and those entities directly or indirectly owned by the Receiving Party, in each case, who shall be informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and shall agree to act in accordance with the terms of this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by it or its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Evaluation' to 'Assessment' introduces ambiguity because the contradicted text refers explicitly to 'Evaluation'. The contradicted text in section (b) states the Receiving Party should only disclose information to Representatives participating in the 'Evaluation'. The change creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether 'Assessment' and 'Evaluation' refer to the same thing, thus creating uncertainty regarding to whom the Receiving Party can disclose information. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition because the contract now uses multiple terms without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein, “Confidential Information” shall mean all information furnished by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (defined below) to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether orally, in writing, electronically or in other tangible form, and identified as confidential or proprietary at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party or otherwise disclosed in a manner such that a reasonable person would understand its confidential nature, including but not limited to, information that is related to: (a) the business plans or operations of the Disclosing Party; (b) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party; (c) the business of any customer or partner of the Disclosing Party; (d) Disclosing Party’s properties, employees, finances, operations; (e) any information about or concerning any third party (which information was provided to the Disclosing Party subject to an applicable confidentiality obligation to such third party); (f) software and related documentation (“Disclosing Party’s Software”) including the following information regarding Disclosing Party’s Software: (i) computer software (object and source codes), programming techniques and programming concepts, methods of processing, system designs embodied in Disclosing Party’s Software; and (ii) discoveries, inventions, concepts, designs, flow charts, documentation, product specifications, application program interface specifications, techniques and processes relating to Disclosing Party’s Software; and (g) product offerings, content partners, product pricing, product availability, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, techniques, formulas, data, schematics, trade secrets, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), marketing plans, forecasts and strategies.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, “Protected Information” shall mean all information furnished by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (defined below) to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether orally, in writing, electronically or in other tangible form, and identified as confidential or proprietary at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party or otherwise disclosed in a manner such that a reasonable person would understand its confidential nature, including but not limited to, information that is related to: (a) the business plans or operations of the Disclosing Party; (b) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party; (c) the business of any customer or partner of the Disclosing Party; (d) Disclosing Party’s properties, employees, finances, operations; (e) any information about or concerning any third party (which information was provided to the Disclosing Party subject to an applicable confidentiality obligation to such third party); (f) software and related documentation (“Disclosing Party’s Software”) including the following information regarding Disclosing Party’s Software: (i) computer software (object and source codes), programming techniques and programming concepts, methods of processing, system designs embodied in Disclosing Party’s Software; and (ii) discoveries, inventions, concepts, designs, flow charts, documentation, product specifications, application program interface specifications, techniques and processes relating to Disclosing Party’s Software; and (g) product offerings, content partners, product pricing, product availability, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, techniques, formulas, data, schematics, trade secrets, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), marketing plans, forecasts and strategies.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Information' but only in the definition, while the rest of the agreement refers to 'Confidential Information', creates a direct conflict. It is unclear which definition to follow or if the terms are equivalent. Specifically, in section 3, the term is used, leading to a direct contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party: (a) shall take all reasonable steps (defined below) to keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential; (b) shall not disclose or reveal any Confidential Information to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of the Evaluation; (c) shall not use Confidential Information for any purpose other than in connection with the Evaluation. and (d) shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of the Evaluation) any information about the Evaluation, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives. As used herein “reasonable steps” means those steps the Receiving Party takes to protect its own similar proprietary and confidential information, which shall not be less than a reasonable standard of care. As used herein. “Representatives” shall mean (i) employees of Receiving Party; (ii) attorneys, accountants, or other professional business advisors and, additionally, (iii) employees of the Receiving Party and those entities directly or indirectly owned by the Receiving Party, in each case, who shall be informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and shall agree to act in accordance with the terms of this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by it or its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information', which is then used in the 'Contradicted Text' section regarding the Receiving Party's obligations. The changed text redefines 'Confidential Information' as 'Protected Information' in the initial definition but does not update the term throughout the document. This creates a direct contradiction as the 'Contradicted Text' section still refers to 'Confidential Information', leaving uncertainty about whether the Receiving Party's obligations apply to 'Protected Information' as defined in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Confidential Information shall not be reproduced in any form except as required to accomplish the intent of this Agreement. Any reproduction of any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party and shall contain any and all confidential or proprietary notices or legends which appear on the original. The Receiving Party: (a) shall take all reasonable steps (defined below) to keep all Confidential Information strictly confidential; (b) shall not disclose or reveal any Confidential Information to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of the Evaluation; (c) shall not use Confidential Information for any purpose other than in connection with the Evaluation. and (d) shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of the Evaluation) any information about the Evaluation, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Confidential Information shall not be reproduced in any form except as required to accomplish the intent of this Agreement. Any reproduction of any Confidential Information of a Disclosing Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party and shall contain any and all confidential or proprietary notices or legends which appear on the original. The Receiving Party: (a) shall take all reasonable steps (defined below) to keep all Confidential Data strictly confidential; (b) shall not disclose or reveal any Confidential Data to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Data for the purpose of the Evaluation; (c) shall not use Confidential Data for any purpose other than in connection with the Evaluation. and (d) shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in the Evaluation or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of the Evaluation) any information about the Evaluation, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Data has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in Section 3 introduces an inconsistency. The document does not define 'Confidential Data', and other sections refer to 'Confidential Information', such as Section 4, creating uncertainty about the scope of the agreement. Specifically, it is unclear if the exceptions in section 4 apply to 'Confidential Data'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The above restrictions on the use or disclosure of the Confidential Information shall not apply to any Confidential Information that: (a) is independently developed by Receiving Party without reference to the Confidential Information, or is lawfully received free of restriction from a third party having the right to furnish such Confidential Information; (b) has become generally available to the public without breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party; (c) at the time of disclosure to Receiving Party was known to such party free of restriction; or (d) Disclosing Party agrees in writing is free of such restrictions.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in Section 3 creates a contradiction because Section 4, the 'Contradicted Text', explicitly refers to 'Confidential Information'. The explanation notes that this change introduces uncertainty about whether the exceptions in Section 4 apply to 'Confidential Data'. Since the document now uses two different terms, and the contradicted text only refers to the original term, there is a strong in-text contradiction according to the Misaligned Terminology definition, as the interchangeable use of 'Confidential Information' and 'Confidential Data' without defining them leads to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The above restrictions on the use or disclosure of the Confidential Information shall not apply to any Confidential Information that: (a) is independently developed by Receiving Party without reference to the Confidential Information, or is lawfully received free of restriction from a third party having the right to furnish such Confidential Information; (b) has become generally available to the public without breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party; (c) at the time of disclosure to Receiving Party was known to such party free of restriction; or (d) Disclosing Party agrees in writing is free of such restrictions.
perturbation.changed_text: The above restrictions on the use or disclosure of the Confidential Information shall not apply to any Proprietary Information that: (a) is independently developed by Receiving Party without reference to the Confidential Information, or is lawfully received free of restriction from a third party having the right to furnish such Confidential Information; (b) has become generally available to the public without breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party; (c) at the time of disclosure to Receiving Party was known to such party free of restriction; or (d) Disclosing Party agrees in writing is free of such restrictions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' at the beginning of section 4 creates confusion because the rest of the section still refers to 'Confidential Information.' It's unclear whether 'Proprietary Information' is meant to include all or part of the defined 'Confidential Information', or if the conditions (a)-(d) apply to one but not the other. This will particularly create contradictions with Section 9, when talking about destroying such information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Receiving Party shall (at the Receiving Party’s election) promptly return or destroy (provided that any such destruction shall be certified by a duly authorized Representative of the Receiving Party) all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party and all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon (whether in hard-copy form or an intangible media, such as electronic mail or computer files) in the Receiving Party’s possession or in the possession of any Representative of the Receiving Party; provided, however: (i) that if a legal proceeding has been instituted to seek disclosure of the Confidential Information, such material shall not be destroyed until the proceeding is settled or a final judgment with respect thereto has been rendered; (ii) that the Receiving Party shall not, in connection with the foregoing obligations, be required to identify or delete Confidential Information held electronically in archive or back-up systems in accordance with general systems archiving or backup policies; and (iii) that the Receiving Party shall not be obligated to return or destroy Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to the extent the Receiving Party is required to retain a copy pursuant to applicable law, and further provided that the Receiving Party will not, and the Receiving Party will use reasonable measures to cause its employees not to, access such Confidential Information so archived or backed-up.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Information' at the beginning of the section outlining exceptions to restrictions on use/disclosure. However, the rest of that section and Section 9 (the contradicted text) still refer to 'Confidential Information.' This creates a contradiction because it's unclear whether the exceptions (a)-(d) in the changed text apply to 'Confidential Information' as referenced in Section 9, or if the obligation to destroy applies to both 'Confidential' and 'Proprietary' information, or just 'Confidential'. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, where the contract uses multiple terms without clarifying their relationship, leading to conflicting obligations, specifically concerning the destruction of information.

perturbation.original_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received material, non-public information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the matters that are the subject of this Agreement, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of securities by any person who has received insider information from the issuer of such securities and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'material, non-public information' to 'insider information' in Section 12 creates ambiguity. While seemingly similar, 'insider information' has a specific legal meaning related to individuals with fiduciary duties. If 'Representatives' interpret this narrowly, they might not consider all 'material, non-public information' to be covered, thus contradicting the broader intent of protecting all such information as defined earlier in the agreement in Section 2. This is because confidential information includes information that is related to: (a) the business plans or operations of the Disclosing Party, and not just insider information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used herein, “Confidential Information” shall mean all information furnished by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (defined below) to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether orally, in writing, electronically or in other tangible form, and identified as confidential or proprietary at the time of disclosure by the Disclosing Party or otherwise disclosed in a manner such that a reasonable person would understand its confidential nature, including but not limited to, information that is related to: (a) the business plans or operations of the Disclosing Party; (b) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party; (c) the business of any customer or partner of the Disclosing Party; (d) Disclosing Party’s properties, employees, finances, operations; (e) any information about or concerning any third party (which information was provided to the Disclosing Party subject to an applicable confidentiality obligation to such third party); (f) software and related documentation (“Disclosing Party’s Software”) including the following information regarding Disclosing Party’s Software: (i) computer software (object and source codes), programming techniques and programming concepts, methods of processing, system designs embodied in Disclosing Party’s Software; and (ii) discoveries, inventions, concepts, designs, flow charts, documentation, product specifications, application program interface specifications, techniques and processes relating to Disclosing Party’s Software; and (g) product offerings, content partners, product pricing, product availability, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, techniques, formulas, data, schematics, trade secrets, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), marketing plans, forecasts and strategies. Where the Confidential Information has not been reduced to written or other tangible form at the time of disclosure, and such disclosure is made orally or visually, the Disclosing Party agrees to identify it as confidential or proprietary at the time of disclosure. “Confidential Information” shall be deemed to include all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives which contain or are based upon, in whole or in part, the information furnished to the receiving party or its Representatives pursuant hereto.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'material, non-public information' to 'insider information' narrows the scope of protected information in the changed text (Section 12). This creates a contradiction with the broader definition of 'Confidential Information' in the contradicted text, which includes 'business plans or operations of the Disclosing Party' and other information beyond just 'insider information' as typically defined. This discrepancy could lead to confusion and inconsistent application of confidentiality obligations, as Representatives might not consider all 'material, non-public information' to be covered, thus contradicting the broader intent of protecting all such information as defined earlier in the agreement in Section 2.
